name: Chía Pass and Corneja Valley Loop
name_original: Puerto de Chia por el Valle del Corneja  — circular desde Vilafranca de la Sierra
description: This difficult 49.0-mile (78.8 km) road cycling route to Puerto de Chia from Vilafranca de la Sierra offers challenging climbs and scenic vi
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Villafranca De La Sierra are situated in the Corneja valley, at the base of the Sierra de Villafranca, within the province of Ávila, Spain. This region offers a diverse landscape characterized by river valleys, meadows, and forests, alongside significant mountain passes. The terrain includes considerable elevation gains, with routes traversing areas like the Sierra de Gredos and the Sierra de la Panamera. Cyclists can expect challenging climbs and descents across varied natural features. Best road cycling routes around Villafranca De La Sierra The most popular road cycling route is Cañada del Horno and Sierra de Gredos Loop, a 47.8 miles (76.9 km) trail that takes 3 hours 55 minutes to complete, featuring significant elevation through the Sierra de Gredos. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Chía Pass and Corneja Valley Loop, a difficult 48.9 miles (78.8 km) path. This route offers panoramic views as it navigates the Chía Pass and the scenic Corneja Valley. Local road cyclists also love the Rodeo a la Sierra de la Panamera — circular por el Puerto de Navalmoral, a 91.2 miles (146.8 km) trail leading through the Sierra de la Panamera, often completed in about 7 hours 20 minutes. Road cycling around Villafranca De La Sierra is defined by mountain passes, river valleys, and the Sierra de Villafranca. The network primarily offers challenging options suitable for road cyclists with very good fitness levels. The routes in Villafranca De La Sierra are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.0 stars from over 4 reviews. More than 400 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Villafranca De La Sierra's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Villafranca De La Sierra?
A: There are over 20 road cycling routes around Villafranca De La Sierra, offering a diverse range of experiences for cyclists. The majority of these routes are classified as difficult, catering to riders with very good fitness levels.

Q: What is the general difficulty level of road cycling routes in Villafranca De La Sierra?
A: Road cycling in Villafranca De La Sierra is predominantly challenging, with 20 out of 21 routes rated as difficult. These routes often feature significant elevation gains and traverse mountain passes, making them suitable for experienced road cyclists seeking demanding climbs and descents.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while road cycling in Villafranca De La Sierra?
A: The region offers a picturesque and varied landscape. You'll cycle through the scenic Corneja valley, flanked by the Sierra de Villafranca, with views of meadows, orchards, and forests of oak and poplar trees. Expect routes along river valleys like the Corneja, Tormes, and Alberche, and challenging ascents over prominent mountain passes.

Q: Are there any notable mountain passes for road cyclists in the region?
A: Yes, Villafranca De La Sierra is known for its challenging mountain passes. Cyclists can tackle the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1711130" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Chía Pass (1,663 m)&lt;/a&gt;, offering panoramic views, or the demanding &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5216726" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Negra Pass&lt;/a&gt;. Other significant passes in the Sierra de Villafranca include Reventón, Santiago, and Fuente Alberche, providing rewarding climbs.

Q: What are some of the longest road cycling routes starting from Villafranca De La Sierra?
A: For those seeking longer distances, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1063037573/rodeo-a-la-sierra-de-la-panamera-circular-por-el-puerto-de-navalmoral" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rodeo a la Sierra de la Panamera — circular por el Puerto de Navalmoral&lt;/a&gt; is a substantial option, covering 91.2 miles (146.8 km) with significant elevation. Another long route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23744674" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Negra Pass – Puerto de Villatoro loop from Puerto de Villatoro&lt;/a&gt;, which is 106.3 km long.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many routes in the area are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1315465827/canada-del-horno-and-sierra-de-gredos-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cañada del Horno and Sierra de Gredos Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1315453509/chia-pass-and-corneja-valley-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Chía Pass and Corneja Valley Loop&lt;/a&gt;, both offering extensive circular tours through the region's stunning landscapes.

Q: What historical or cultural attractions can I find near the cycling routes?
A: Beyond the natural beauty, Villafranca De La Sierra boasts a rich history. You can explore its medieval origin with a main square featuring heraldic houses, the 15th-century Church of Nuestra Señora de la Asunción, and remnants of a ruined fortress. The town also has one of the oldest bullrings in Ávila, dating back to 1853.

Q: Are there any specific viewpoints or scenic spots along the road cycling routes?
A: The N-110 road, especially leading to the Puerto de Chía, offers breathtaking panoramic views of the multi-colored Corneja valley and the rich pastures of La Serrota and the Sierra de Villafranca. Mountain passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5216726" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Negra Pass&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1711130" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Chía Pass (1,663 m)&lt;/a&gt; are also prime locations for stunning vistas.

Q: What do other cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Villafranca De La Sierra?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the challenging nature of the routes, the stunning mountainous landscapes, and the rewarding climbs over passes like Chía and Peña Negra, which offer incredible views.

Q: Is there a waterfall accessible near the cycling routes?
A: Yes, a beautiful waterfall formed by the Arroyo de las Malezas can be found within the municipality. It's accessible via a route that begins near the 'Molino del Tío Alberto', which is the sole remaining mill of what was once an extensive route of over 20 mills along the Corneja river.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Villafranca De La Sierra?
A: While specific seasonal data isn't provided, the diverse terrain and mountain passes suggest that spring and autumn would likely offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling, avoiding the extreme heat of summer and potential snow or ice in winter at higher elevations. Always check local weather forecasts before planning your ride.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through the Sierra de Gredos?
A: Yes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1315465827/canada-del-horno-and-sierra-de-gredos-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cañada del Horno and Sierra de Gredos Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a popular route that takes you through the Sierra de Gredos, offering significant elevation and a challenging ride through this renowned mountain range.
tips: With an altitude of 1663 meters, this mountain pass is located at the point that connects two valleys, the Valle del Corneja (Valdecorneja) with the Alto Valle del Alberche.

The relief of the municipality is formed by two mountainous areas separated by the Amblés valley, through which the Adaja river runs, which is born in the territory. The northern area is called Sierra de Villanueva, a branch of the Sierra de Ávila.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Puerto de Chia por el Valle del Corneja road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is categorized as difficult, demanding a very good level of physical fitness. It covers 78.8 kilometers with a significant accumulated elevation gain of 1,250 meters, indicating sustained climbs, particularly to the Chía Pass.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily features firm pavement, making it ideal for road bikes. You will cycle approximately 52.5 kilometers on local roads and 25.8 kilometers on national roads, offering a varied cycling experience through the region.

faqs.question: Where can I park when starting this circular route in Vilafranca de la Sierra?
faqs.answer: As the route is circular and starts and finishes in Vilafranca de la Sierra, you can typically find parking within the village. Look for designated public parking areas or suitable street parking close to the town center to begin your ride conveniently.

faqs.question: Are there any specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle in the Sierras de la Paramera y la Serrota Natural Space?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public roads within the Sierras de la Paramera y la Serrota Natural Space. However, always respect local regulations, especially concerning protected areas and private property.

faqs.question: What are the best times of year to cycle the Puerto de Chia por el Valle del Corneja route?
faqs.answer: The best times to cycle this route are typically during the spring (April to June) and autumn (September to October) months. During these periods, the weather is generally milder and more stable, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the colder, potentially icy conditions of winter.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its difficult rating, significant distance (78.8 km), and substantial elevation gain (1,250 m), this route is not recommended for families with young children or beginner cyclists. It requires a very good level of physical fitness and experience with challenging road climbs.

faqs.question: What natural features and viewpoints can I expect along the route?
faqs.answer: You can expect picturesque landscapes characteristic of the Valle del Corneja and the Sierra de Ávila. The route offers views of cereal plains alongside the Corneja River and expansive mountain vistas on the horizon, especially as you ascend towards the Chía Pass. The route also passes through mountainous areas, adding to its visual appeal.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with any other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several official trails. You will encounter sections of the [CIMA AV01] Chía, Cañada Real Leonesa Occidental, Pontón de los Tomillos, and Camino Villafranca - San Miguel de Corneja during your ride.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the Puerto de Chia por el Valle del Corneja cycling route?
faqs.answer: As this is primarily a road cycling route, dogs are generally allowed if they are safely secured (e.g., in a bike trailer or basket) and do not interfere with traffic or other cyclists. However, it's important to be mindful of local regulations and ensure your dog's safety on public roads.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or restaurants available along the route or in Vilafranca de la Sierra?
faqs.answer: Vilafranca de la Sierra, being the start and end point, offers amenities such as cafes and restaurants where you can refuel before or after your ride. The route also passes through Villatoro, which may have limited options. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially for the longer, more remote sections.

faqs.question: What is a key challenge or insider tip for tackling the Puerto de Chia por el Valle del Corneja route?
faqs.answer: The most significant challenge and a key feature of this route is the sustained climb to the Chía Pass. To best enjoy this demanding section, ensure you are well-hydrated and have paced yourself during the earlier parts of the ride. Starting early in the morning can help you avoid midday heat, especially during warmer months.
